General safety information
Important safety instructions
1.1 Important safety instructions
It is important to install, operate, maintain, and dispose of the equipment according to the
information in the product documentation, and in compliance with local regulations. For the
safety of the users and the patients, before a user operates or works on the equipment, they
must read, understand, and obey all:
• Warnings, cautions, instructions, and notes in this document and the related
documents.
• Safety instructions, emergency procedures, and information in this document and the
related documents.
• Warnings, cautions, and safety markings on the equipment and the accessories of the
equipment.
If you have local work instructions for the equipment, then Elekta recommends that you include
the warnings, cautions, and notes from this document in your local work instructions.
1.1.1 Conventions for warnings, cautions, and notes
The conventions give information about the class and the level of the risk in the warnings and
cautions, and the content of notes.
WARNING 1.1
A warning is information about procedures, tasks, or conditions with a risk of fatal or serious
injury, injury, or clinical mistreatment, if you do not obey the instructions.
CAUTION 1.1
A caution is information about procedures, tasks, or conditions with a risk of damage to the
equipment, data, or the environment, if you do not obey the instructions.
Note: A note gives more information about a related text.
1.1.2 Expected service life of the equipment
The expected service life is the length of time that the regular service and repair procedures can
keep the equipment safe to operate.
WARNING 1.2
Do not operate the equipment unless you correctly complete the safety checks, planned
maintenance schedules, and extended life checks. If you ignore this warning, you can cause
fatal injury and damage to the equipment.
The expected service life of this equipment is 15 years of normal use.
The Elekta definition for 'expected service life' is the length of time that the regular service and
repair procedures can keep the equipment safe to operate.
The Elekta definition of 'normal use' is the treatment of 50 patients each day for five days each
week of the year. If the equipment is used more frequently than this, the frequency of the
planned maintenance must increase accordingly and the extended life checks must also be
brought forward accordingly.

General safety information
Important safety instructions
The level of operation of the equipment can be different to ‘normal use’. This has an effect on the
length of the service life. Because of wear of parts, the risk of a malfunction can increase after 10
years operation. Elekta recommends that before you continue operation after 10 years, you
complete the regular extended life checks.
Elekta can help the User with the correct safety checks, planned maintenance schedules, and the
regular extended life checks for continued safe operation of the equipment.
The system must be maintained throughout its expected service life in compliance with the EMC
information detailed in the applicable Instructions for Use.
For information on the above checks and schedules, contact your local Elekta representative.

1.2 Safety precautions
1.2.1 Safety precautions for the compatibility of the equipment
WARNING 1.3
Do not use parts, accessories, or other equipment, without approval by Elekta of their
compatibility. If you ignore this warning, you can cause fatal injury and damage to the
equipment.
Only operate the equipment with Elekta supplied or approved, compatible equipment or parts.
Accessories, transducers, and cables that are not specified by or do not have the approval of
Elekta can cause an incorrect treatment delivery. They can also have an effect on the performance
of the electromagnetic compatibility (EMC), which can increase the emissions or decrease the
immunity of the equipment.
Contact Elekta for information about the compatibility of other equipment or parts.
1.2.2 Safety precautions for the electromagnetic compatibility
The design and manufacture of Elekta products is in compliance with applicable EMC standards.
The intended result of the standards is to minimize the risk of electromagnetic interference by RF
devices. The type of device and the distance to the equipment has an effect on the intensity of
the electromagnetic interference. The equipment includes the linear accelerator and accessories,
the control cabinets, the computers in the control room, and equipment from other
manufacturer.
Document ID: 4513 370 2096_10 Elekta Medical Linear Accelerator

Page 10 © Elekta Group 2022. All rights reserved.

General safety information
Safety precautions
It is necessary to have special precautions for EMC emissions. Elekta recommends that you obey
these precautions when you use RF devices near the equipment.
• Do not use RF devices unless they are in compliance with international standards for
electromagnetic compatibility, for example, the CE or FCC mark.
• Do not use a two-way emergency radio in the treatment room or the control room.
Only use an emergency radio if there is an emergency.
• Do not use cellular telephones, or other devices with cellular modems near the
equipment. The Instructions for Use contains information on the minimum safe
distance of these devices from the equipment.
• When you use a device that is enabled for wireless operation, it must be a minimum of
10 cm from the equipment.
• Radio-Frequency Identification (RFID) readers, when activated to do a scan of a RFID
tag, must be a minimum of one meter from the equipment.
Note: The RFID systems that Elekta supply use lower power levels. You can safely use these RFID systems
when you obey the procedures in the Instructions for Use for the product.
The system must be installed, put into service, and maintained in compliance with the EMC
information in the Instructions for Use.
Contact Elekta for information about the compatibility of parts and equipment.
WARNING 1.4
Do not put portable or mobile radio frequency (RF) communication devices near to the
equipment. The emissions from such RF devices can be more than the EMC standards, and can
have unwanted effects on the equipment operation. If you ignore this warning, the RF
emissions can cause the equipment to have a malfunction. A malfunction can cause fatal injury,
clinical mistreatment, or damage to the equipment.
WARNING 1.5
Do not use accessories, transducers, and cables that are not specified by Elekta. They can have
an effect on the EMC performance, which can increase EMC emissions or decrease the
immunity to RF interference of the equipment. If you ignore this warning, incorrect radiation
delivery and other malfunctions can occur, which can cause fatal injury or clinical mistreatment.
1.2.3 Safety precaution for Service Tools and lifting equipment
WARNING 1.6
Do not use defective or damaged lifting equipment. If you ignore this warning, you can cause
fatal injury and damage to the equipment.
WARNING 1.7
Do not use incorrectly calibrated or defective Service Tools. If you ignore this warning, you can
cause injury and damage to the equipment.
Before you use the Service Tool or lifting equipment, make sure that:
• There are no defective or damaged parts
• The calibration of the Service Tool is correct

• The applicable calibration certificate is valid
• The lifting equipment gives the maximum Safe Working Load (SWL).
For information on the maintenance and calibration schedules of the Service Tool, see the

1.2.4 Safety precaution for work with electrical and mechanical parts
WARNING 1.8
Do not connect any electrical power supply before the ground earth connections are secure.
There is a risk of high leakage current. If you ignore this warning, you can cause fatal injury.
WARNING 1.9
Do not remove the cables or the covers, or operate the equipment without the covers. If you
ignore this warning, you can cause fatal injury
The covers on the equipment prevent access to high voltage parts and parts that move. If you
remove the covers, there is a risk of high voltage shock or injury from parts that move.
Only remove the covers if you are an Elekta approved person with the correct authority and you
obey the applicable safety procedures.
1.2.5 Safety precaution for work with safety devices during service
operation
WARNING 1.10
Do not remove, change, or override the safety switches, interlocks, and other safety devices. If
you ignore this warning, you can cause fatal injury or clinical mistreatment.
The procedures in this document can tell you to remove, change, or override the safety switches,
interlocks, or other safety devices during repair or maintenance work. When the work is
completed, make all the safety devices active again, and do a test of them.
1.2.6 Safety precaution for work on BLDs with UV lighting
WARNING 1.11
Do not look into the ultraviolet lighting of the BLD without protective glasses. If you ignore this
warning, you can cause serious injury.
The covers and screens of the BLD prevent an injury to your eyes from the ultraviolet lighting.
Before you remove the covers or screens of the BLD, switch off the linear accelerator, or use
protective glasses. Protective glasses must have the UV-A specification OD 6+ @ 190 - 532 Nm.
If there is damage to the covers or screen of the BLD, switch off the linear accelerator and contact
the Elekta representative.

General safety information
Safety precautions
1.2.7 Safety precaution for work with lasers
WARNING 1.12
Do not look into the laser beams because they can cause damage to your eyes. If you ignore
this warning, you can cause serious injury.
Elekta systems use the laser beams to help set up patients in the correct position for treatment.
During your work in the treatment room, make sure that you do not look into the laser beams.
1.2.8 Safety precautions for work with display screen equipment
WARNING 1.13
Do not use the display screen equipment for long periods of time. If you ignore this warning,
you can cause injury.
When you use the display screen equipment (DSE), obey the applicable Health and Safety
regulations and procedures in the jurisdiction where you do the work.
1.2.9 Safety precautions for cleaning and disinfection
WARNING 1.14
Do not clean the equipment, or use a disinfectant spray when the equipment is connected to
the electrical supply. If you ignore this warning, you can cause fatal injury.
WARNING 1.15
Do not use a spray that is explosive or flammable. If you ignore this warning, you can cause
fatal injury and damage to the equipment.
CAUTION 1.2
Do not let water and other liquids go into the equipment. If you ignore this caution, you can
cause damage to the equipment.
CAUTION 1.3
Do not use a spray to clean the medical equipment room. If you ignore this caution, you can
cause damage to the equipment.
At regular intervals, it is necessary to clean the equipment and equipment room with
disinfectants. Elekta recommends that the procedures to clean the equipment and the equipment
room obey the applicable laws and regulations of the jurisdiction where the equipment is
installed.

General safety information
Safety precautions
1.2.10 Safety precautions for work with high temperatures on the BLD
covers
During normal operation, the maximum temperatures of the accessory ring and BLD outer covers
can increase to 45 °C. Make sure that you do not touch the outer covers or the accessory ring for
more than 1 minute.
1.2.11 Safety precaution for the installation of accessories on the BLD
WARNING 1.16
Do not touch the connector found in the accessory ring on the BLD. If you ignore this warning,
you can cause injury.
When you install an accessory, for example, a shadow tray, electron applicator, or front pointer,
do not touch the pins in the connector. The pins have a low electrical current, which can give an
electric shock when you touch them.
1.2.12 Safety precautions for work with heavy parts
The safety precautions for work with heavy parts are the necessary safety precautions to know to
prevent injury.
WARNING 1.17
Do not lift or move heavy parts unless you obey the applicable Health and Safety regulations
and procedures. If you ignore this warning, you can cause serious injury.
1.2.13 Safety precautions during work on the BLD
When you do work on the BLD, make sure that you obey the following safety precautions:
WARNING 1.18
Do not do work on the inner parts of the beam limiting device while the electrical supply is
connected. There is a risk of high voltage electric shock. If you ignore this warning, you can
cause fatal injury.
WARNING 1.19
Do not do work on the beam limiting device unless the gantry is at 0° or 180°. Gravity can cause
the leaves, diaphragms, and dynamic leaf guides to move. If you ignore this warning, you can
cause fatal injury.
WARNING 1.20
Do not touch the hot LED field light projector, or the heat sink, immediately after operation. If
you ignore this warning, you can cause serious injury.

General safety information
Safety precautions
1.2.14 Safety precaution for the removal of parts from the radiation head
WARNING 1.21
Do not touch the flight tube, bending magnet, or other parts near to them until a check for
induced radioactivity is completed. Excessive exposure to radioactivity is dangerous. If you
ignore this warning, you can cause serious injury.
After the operation of the linear accelerator at high energy, the parts in the radiation head can
become radioactive and a radiation hazard. Before you do work on, or remove parts from the
radiation head, use a calibrated radiation detector to measure the level of induced radioactivity.
Use the applicable safety procedures when you remove radioactive parts and material. The
maximum dose rate for the safe removal of radioactive parts and material is 50 µSv/hr
(microsievert per hour). Do not remove the radioactive parts and material from the treatment
room before they are correctly prepared for safe removal.
1.2.15 Safety precautions for maintenance, modification, and
decommissioning
WARNING 1.22
Do not do work, or make modifications to the equipment without the correct training and
authority. If you ignore this warning, you can cause fatal injury, clinical mistreatment, and
damage to the equipment.
Only do work on, or make modifications to, the equipment if you are an Elekta approved person
with the applicable authority. Such work must use best practice and obey the laws and
regulations in the jurisdiction in which the equipment is installed.
Modifications or work that does not have approval by Elekta, and is not done by an Elekta
approved person with the correct authority, can cause:
• Fatal injury
• Incorrect radiation delivery
• Malfunction of the equipment
• Damage to the equipment
• The warranty to be canceled.
1.2.16 Safety precautions for work in areas of induced radioactivity
The safety precautions for work in areas of induced radioactivity are the precautions to prevent
an excessive exposure to, and serious injury from, induced radioactivity.
When you operate or do work on the equipment, Elekta recommends that you use the applicable
radiation protection functions, devices, systems, procedures, and accessories. Always obey the
applicable laws and regulations for induced radioactivity for the jurisdiction in which the
equipment is installed.
The safety precautions are applicable to:
• Maintenance, modifications, and decommissioning
• Measurement of the induced radioactivity
• Removal and movement of parts with induced radioactivity.
Elekta Medical Linear Accelerator Document ID: 4513 370 2096_10
Corrective Maintenance Manual for the Movements System
© Elekta Group 2022. All rights reserved. Page 15

General safety information
Safety precautions
1.2.16.1 Safety precautions for the measurement of induced radioactivity
The safety precautions for the measurement of induced radioactivity are the necessary safety
precautions to know to prevent excessive exposure to radioactivity, which can cause serious
injury or damage to the environment.
Always use two radiation detectors to measure the level of induced radioactivity. Only use
radiation detectors that have an applicable calibration certificate. The period of time since the
last calibration date must be less than 12 months. Do not use the radiation detectors if the period
of time since the last calibration date is more than 12 months.
WARNING 1.23
Do not move radioactive material (by air, sea, or land) unless you obey the applicable laws and
regulations for induced radioactivity and disposal of radioactive material of the jurisdictions
through which the movement occurs. If you ignore this warning, you can cause serious injury
and damage to the environment.
1.2.16.2 Safety precautions for handling parts with induced radioactivity
The handling of parts with induced radioactivity must comply with the laws and regulations of the
jurisdiction in which the equipment is installed. The safe levels of radiation applicable in the UK
do not apply in all jurisdictions. The permitted level of radioactivity must always be as low as
reasonably achievable (ALARA). This level may vary depending on local rules, therefore the safe
working levels from the applicable local rules must be considered as the acceptable working limit.
If there are no safe levels available for a jurisdiction, use the UK value as a guideline, which is a
maximum of 150 cps at the surface for a maximum duration of 72 minutes. The following table
shows the conversion from this value to other units.
Table 1.2 Conversion between the units of induced radioactivity
Counts per second Microsieverts per Milliroentgen per
(cps) hour (µSv/hr) hour (mR/hr)
150 50 5
For information on the safe removal of parts with induced radioactivity and the safe levels of
radiation for your jurisdiction, contact Elekta or a Qualified Expert (QE).
1.3 Emergencies
1.3.1 Safety precaution for the occurrence of fire or explosion
WARNING 1.24
Do not use the equipment in rooms that contain explosive or flammable gases. If you ignore
this warning, you can cause fatal injury and damage to the equipment.
High levels of oxygen, and some anesthetic gases and disinfectant sprays, can make an explosive
or flammable environment in the room.

General safety information
Emergencies
1.3.2 Recommendations for emergency procedures
Before you use the equipment, Elekta recommends that you know all the local emergency
procedures and the locations of all the emergency switches.
The Elekta equipment includes:
• The STOP MOTORS buttons on the handheld controllers and user interface panels in
the treatment room, and FKP in the control room.
When you press these buttons, it disconnects the electrical supply from the motors,
stops all movements of the equipment, and stops kV and MV radiation.
• The EMERGENCY POWER OFF buttons in the treatment room, control area, and areas
near the equipment.
When you operate these buttons, it disconnects the electrical supply to the linear
accelerator. The buttons disconnect the electrical supply from many electrical circuits,
but not from all the circuits.

2.1.2 Recommendations for training
The regulations for training give a description of the necessary training, regulations, and

Different countries have different regulations for training. Make sure that you have the necessary
training before you operate or do work on the equipment. Make sure that your training is in
compliance with the laws and regulations of the jurisdiction in which the equipment is installed.
Training is available from Elekta. Contact Elekta for more information about the applicable
training courses.
2.1.3 Disposal of the equipment
End of life (EOL) disposal is the procedure to disassemble, remove, and discard the equipment, in
full or in parts, to a condition where it is not possible to operate the equipment for its intended
use.
Only a Qualified Expert (QE) and an approved facility with an applicable license, may remove and
discard the equipment and recycle the material.
A full description of disposal requirements is detailed in a separate document Product Disposal
Information. This document is available on request from Elekta.
The customer owns the data on the storage media. If there are any data storage media such as
HDDs, remove them from the device and hand them to the customer for disposal. If the customer
entrusts the disposal team to dispose of the data, the data must be destroyed in accordance with
relevant cybersecurity regulations.

Introduction
Document scope
3.1 Document scope
The corrective maintenance manual (CMM) helps service engineers troubleshoot, repair and
maintain the systems, subsystems and components of the linear accelerator. The target audience
is qualified service engineers. These may be engineers employed directly by Elekta, qualified
representatives of Elekta or qualified representatives of Elekta's customers.
The manual provides:
• A description of the system or sub-system in question.
• A troubleshooting section to identify the problem areas.
• A set of detailed procedures used to replace FRUs (field replaceable units).
• A section on how to return the device to work.
If you find any mistakes in this document, or identify areas for improvement, please raise a CLM
(customer lifecycle management) case and assign it to the relevant queue.

Product Description of the Movement Systems
Description of the movement assemblies of the linear accelerator
4.1 Description of the movement assemblies of the linear
accelerator
The gantry of the linear accelerator has a dedicated movement system that controls the rotation
of the gantry drum. The diaphragm assembly has a dedicated movement system that controls its
rotation. The gantry and diaphragm movements are known together as machine movements.
In the asymmetric diaphragms and MLCi/MLCi2, each diaphragm (X1, X2, Y1 and Y2) has a
movement system that moves it into and out of the radiation beam. These movement systems
are not installed on the Beam Modulator™.
On the Agility™ system, a MLC power supply unit (PSU) controls the diaphragm movement
system.
For calibration, the movement system of the treatmennt table gives an input to the control
software of the linear accelerator.
The movement assemblies have many directions of movement. The assemblies rotate about axes
to give an intersection at the isocenter of the radiation beam. The movement control system
accurately monitors these positions during movement.
Apart from the movement control system for diaphragm rotation, refer to the applicable
corrective maintenance manual for more information about the BLD.
For more information, see the applicable corrective maintenance manual for more information
about the treatment table.
4.1.1 Operation of a movement system
The operation of each movement system is almost the same. One or more of the electric motors
operate the movement assemblies. The user uses a manual controller to tell the movement
system that a movement is necessary. This enables the movement. The speed and direction of the
movement are set by software control items as signals from the control processor of the TCS. The
command signals of the related control and motor drive areas operate the applicable motor.
The encoders monitor the current position of the assembly during the movement. A servo loop
sends this information to the TCS as software items. The control software can therefore start and
stop the movement and adjust the speed and direction of movement in real time.

Description of the manual controllers
Each servo item accepts a number of inputs and contains an algorithm to find out which of the
modes (and therefore which of the inputs) to use. These inputs can be from ASU (machine
movements only), autotrack (diaphragms only), or thumbwheel control.
In Arc Therapy mode (gantry only), and Dynamic Therapy mode some items are set by the
parameters of the prescription.
4.2 Description of the manual controllers
The linear accelerator has a number of devices that let users manually control the different
movement systems. They are:
• Handheld controllers (HHC)
• Function keypad (FKP)
• BLD control units (HCUs)
• User interface modules on the treatment table.
Note: There are two different types of FKP. The earlier FKP version is the legacy FKP and the later FKP
version is the ‘new FKP’.
The manual controllers supply the input signals to the control software. This puts the inputs
together to give enable, speed and direction commands to the movement systems.
See the applicable treatment table corrective maintenance manual for more information on the
user interface modules.
4.2.1 Handheld controller type 1
The type of handheld controller (HHC) depends on the configuration of the linear accelerator.
The handheld controller (HHC) lets you control:
• Mechanical functions of the system, for example, gantry rotation, collimator rotation,
treatment table movement, and field settings.
• Treatment room monitor (TRM) page displays, treatment room lights, optical distance
indicator (ODI), and laser light settings.
• Linear accelerator and table automatic setup (ASU).
• Remote automatic table movements (RATM).

4.2.7 Description of the head control unit for the beam limiting device
Each of the types of BLD installed on Elekta linear accelerators has a different set of head control
units (HCUs):
• The asymmetric diaphragms and MLCi/MLCi2 HCUs have thumbwheels and buttons to
rotate the BLD assembly.
• The Beam Modulator™ does not have a diaphragm movement system, but has two
HCUs. The controls let the user manually rotate the BLD assembly.
4.3 Description of automatic setup
ASU moves the gantry, BLD, diaphragms, and the table isocentric rotation automatically to the
prescribed positions. ASU also lets you automatically retract the MV detector panel (for new FKPs
only). You can operate ASU with the FKP in the control room, or with the HHC or the user
interface module in the treatment room. Table ASU is optional and does not operate unless it is a
licensed option.
For Elekta Harmony systems, in the treatment room, Linac ASU is available on the HHC and Table
ASU is available on the user interface module. Table isocentric rotation is not applicable for Elekta
Harmony systems.
4.3.1 Description of ASU for the treatment table and the linear
accelerator
You can enable or disable the ASU operation of the FKP, the HHC and the user interface module in
the Linac Options tab (see Figure 4.7). Each of the controllers is configured for ASU of the gantry
and/or the treatment table. The rotation of the BLD is always enabled, together with the
diaphragms and leaves in a BLD.

In ASU mode, the position of each machine movement is derived from the treatment prescription
and held in software. The movements operate until the difference between the set position and
the current position are in tolerance.
The maximum speed of each movement is defined by the control software. You can adjust the
percentage of the maximum speed that is set by the ASU from the control terminal or at the HHC.
During ASU movement, the speed decreases automatically to a nominal 50% of maximum when:
• The rotational movement assembly is at an angle of 5° before the necessary stop angle
• The linear movement assembly is at a position that is 25 mm before the necessary stop
position.

Description of the software control of the movement system
ASU button on the user interface module controls the ASU movements, and the movement speed
is not adjustable. This is through software Item 20.
In the control room, you start ASU when you press the ENABLE key and the Linac ASU, or the
Table ASU buttons. The speed percentage used, is a previous value input at the keyboard.
When you start ASU in the treatment room or the control room, the gantry and the BLD move to
the position at the same time, or the table moves to its position. You cannot move the gantry and
BLD at the same time as the table.
ASU does not operate from the FKP and HHC or user interface module when:
• In Clinical Mode, an electron applicator is installed on the BLD
• The table clutch inhibit is activated, or any of the table PSS brakes are disengaged.
ASU from the FKP is not possible when:
• In Clinical mode, touchguard override is selected.
• In Clinical Mode, a shadow tray with a specified code is installed (as specified in the
Linac options page of the Configuration tool for a Moduleaf, if applicable).
• The Room Doors 2 interlock (Item 259 Rm.doors 2) is open (Doors 2 interlock is service
room door two, also known as the engineer’s door).
The interlock with Room Door 2 stops rotation of the gantry from the control room while an
engineer is behind the gantry. If the service access door is configured differently, there must be
local procedures to prevent this possible accident.
During ASU from the FKP, movements stop if you remove an accessory. Movements do not start
until you release and press the ASU button on the FKP again.
ASU from the FKP is disabled when there is a demand from the treatment room for motor
movement. This includes demands from the BLD controls, table pod, and HHC or user interface
module. When the demand is made from the HHC or user interface module, it is not necessary to
select the movement ENABLE bars on the HHC or user interface module to disable the ASU from
the FKP. ASU from the FKP is disabled until the ASU key on the FKP is released and pressed again.

signals to a selected movement.

In the asymmetric diaphragms and MLCi™/MLCi2™, the diaphragm pairs can move one at a time

software items for each pair of diaphragms.

The Beam Modulator™ does not have moving diaphragms. Therefore, if autotracking is necessary
it must be done with the leaves.

4.6 Description of the movement systems servo loop
The servo loops have a pair of encoders that send information about the movement system
position to the TCS. The encoders are:
• The primary encoder which has two potentiometers (coarse and fine) that give the TCS
information about position.
• The secondary encoder gives the secondary display of the values. This has a check
potentiometer. This monitors the status of the other two potentiometers.
Each of the potentiometers is directly connected to the movement assembly. The voltages read
from the potentiometers are therefore in proportion to the position of the assembly. The
encoders digitize the voltages and then send them to the TCS as the feedback of the servo loop.
The gantry and diaphragm movement systems use all the primary and secondary readouts. The
control system reads only the coarse potentiometer of the table movement system for calibration
functions.
See Table 4.8 for information about the movement system encoder potentiometers.

4.6.1 Description of the movement system coarse potentiometer
The coarse potentiometer supplies the primary position information for the movement system to
the TCS. It is a one wiper device that rotates approximately 10 times through the full range of the
movement. See Figure 4.9 for the waveform of the output voltage.
The output of the coarse potentiometer lets the TCS calculate the number of rotations of the fine
potentiometer. It also identifies if the wiper A or wiper B signal from the fine potentiometer is the
data source.
The voltage graph of the coarse and check potentiometer are straight lines in the approximate
range 0 V to 10 V. The position of the potentiometers on the gear shaft sets the value. There is
usually a small offset between the two indications of the potentiometer.

The fine potentiometer supplies the primary position information for the movement system to

The track of the potentiometer is less than 360°, therefore the fine potentiometer has two
wipers. Wiper A and wiper B are 180° out of phase with each other. This gives a difference of
approximately 5 V between the wiper A voltage and the wiper B voltage. One of the wipers is
always in the 180° sector of the track that does not contain the end stops. The TCS uses the
output of this wiper, between 2.5 V and 7.5 V, to get a stable signal to derive the movement
position.
4.6.3 Description of the movement system check potentiometer
The check potentiometer is part of the secondary encoder and therefore operates independently
from the primary encoder. It is an one wiper device that rotates approximately 10 times through
the full range of the movement. The secondary encoder sends this information to the TCS.
The check potentiometer operates independently of the primary encoder. The signal path of the
check potentiometer is isolated from the signals of the coarse and fine potentiometers.
If the operation of the secondary encoder stops, or an electronic fault occurs, there is a difference
between the primary and secondary encoder voltages. This difference in the encoder voltages
change the linear accelerator to a closed state (no movements). It also prevents the linear
accelerator from entry to the Preparatory machine state until the difference is cleared.
4.6.4 Description of the positional resolution of the movement encoder
The fine and coarse potentiometers on the primary encoder monitor the gantry position. The
gantry display uses the coarse potentiometer to give the gantry angle at a resolution of
approximately 36° /V. The coarse potentiometer also points to one of the curves of the fine
potentiometer, which gives the gantry angle at a resolution of 1.8° /V. The display system can
therefore show the gantry angle to two decimal places.

4.6.5 Description of the drive gear ratios of the potentiometers
In the gantry movement system, there is a drive gear ratio of 1:2 between the coarse
potentiometer and the fine potentiometer. As the gantry moves through 360°, the output of the
fine potentiometer scans through 200 V (20 × 10 V). This gives an accuracy of the display and the
signal-to-noise ratio of approximately 0.05%.
In the diaphragm movement system, there is a drive gear ratio of 1:1 between the coarse
potentiometer and the fine potentiometer. The fine potentiometer therefore rotates 10 times for
a full rotation of the movement. For diaphragm movements, display accuracy and the signal-to-
noise ratio are approximately 0.1%.
4.6.6 Description of the Learn mode
For the control software to calculate the position of a movement, it must know how the curves of
the three potentiometers are related. To do this, the movement operates in Learn mode, which
calculates the slope and point of intersection of each curve. Therefore the control software
knows:
• The correct curve of the fine potentiometer to use for the coarse potentiometer
voltage
• How the voltage output from the check potentiometer voltage is related to the voltage
from the coarse potentiometer.
The TCS keeps three performance indicators for each movement system which measure:
• The error in the potentiometer correlation of the fine potentiometer
• The error in the phase between the fine potentiometer in relation to the coarse
potentiometer
• The error between the check potentiometer and the coarse potentiometer.
Before calibration, the non-linear tracks of the potentiometers must be correlated. The Learn
mode derives a number of correlation coefficients as the movement system moves between its
end points. The coefficients derived are specified to one set of potentiometers. Therefore, you
must do the correlation procedure when you install a new potentiometer.
Item 135 Gant chk Part156 shows the correlation error for a specified movement of the check
potentiometer. This shows the maximum difference between the coarse and check
potentiometers.
4.6.7 Description of the calibration of the movement system position
When you do a calibration, the related software item must be set to manual control. This
decreases the movement speed, overrides the software limits and ignores the position errors.
The action mask is set to override potentiometer correlation errors. This puts the linear
accelerator in the Preparatory machine state and lets movements occur. You can then do the
potentiometer correlation in Learn mode to calibrate the position of the movement system.
4.7 Description of the power supplies for the movement
system motors
There are two motor drive PSUs which supply ±26 V DC power supplies to the diaphragm and
rotation motors.

4.8 Description of the movement systems interlock
The interlock system for the linear accelerator includes the electrical power supplies to the motor
drive circuits of the movement systems. The software interlocks give the patient protection
against injury, and the linear accelerator protection against damage. These software interlocks
give the signals for servo control items and the universal motor drive (UMD) PCBs.
The movement system interlock has two contactor circuits. One circuit operates CON-B and the
other circuit CON-C. The contactors are connected in series and control the primary electrical
supply to the 26 V power supply circuits of the movement system motors.
The stop motors and emergency off switches control the CON-B interlock circuit. The gantry
movement limit switches, treatment table height movement limit switches, and the touchguard
interlock control the CON-C chain.
If it is necessary, you can override the touchguard interlock. Operate the touchguard override
switch on the HHC or the the user interface modules of the treatment table to override the
interlock.
Refer to the Elekta Medical Linear Accelerator Corrective Maintenance Manual for the Power
Supplies and Interlocks for more information about the power supplies and interlocks.

4.9.1 Description of the gantry motors
Elekta linear accelerators have two motors installed in the gantry base (Area 50S). Each of the
motors rotates a separate support wheel.
4.9.2 Operation of the gantry movement system
The gantry movement system (see Figure 4.12) can rotate the gantry through 360° to deliver
radiation to the prescribed position for treatment. An electric motor and drive belts move two
drive wheels to rotate the gantry to the specified position. The jockey wheels keep the gantry
drum in a stable position during rotation.

The rotation of the gantry is with two of the four wheels on the gantry base. The two drive

An electric motor and belt moves each of the wheels. The drive mechanism has a gearbox and a

make sure that there is no forward and back movement of the gantry drum as it rotates. When

4.9.4 Description of the primary encoder of the gantry movement system
The primary encoder is installed on a bracket which contains the coarse and fine potentiometers
and the drive gears. The primary encoder engages with the front toothed drive belt. As the gantry
drum rotates, the drive belt transfers the small movement of the gantry drum to the
potentiometers through the drive gears.
The terminals on the fine potentiometer (RV1) supply the analog voltage outputs that
subsequently become items Item 573 Gant Fin A and Item 586 Gant Fin B. A terminal on the
coarse potentiometer (RV2) supplies the voltage for Item 560 Gant coars. These signals are
routed to the SCC-ICB PCB and the AI12 PCBs in the ICCA.
See the Elekta Oncology Products Service Parts Catalog for more information about the
mechanical assemblies of the gantry movements.
4.9.5 Description of the secondary encoder of the gantry movement
system
The secondary encoder is installed on a bracket which contains the check potentiometer and its
drive gears. The secondary encoder engages with the rear toothed drive belt. As the gantry drum
rotates, the drive belt transfers small movements of the gantry drum through the drive gears to
the check potentiometer.
A terminal on the check potentiometer (RV3) supplies the voltage for Item 135 Gant chk. This is
routed to the SCC-ICA PCB and the AI12 PCBs in the ICCA.
See the Elekta Oncology Products-Service Parts Catalog for more information about the
mechanical assemblies of the gantry movements.

Table 4.13 Operation of the gantry movement systems
Item Description
Control signal The software multiplexer gets the gantry control Item 148 Gant ctrl from the
generation TCS, together with the data about the gantry position from the encoders.
This gives a SPEED/DIRECTION command and discrete ENABLE and BRAKE
signals.
The multiplexer sends the 8-bit SPEED/DIRECTION signal to the AO8-ICCA
PCB, which changes the digital input data to an analog drive signal. The AO8-
ICCA PCB sends the analog drive signal to the UMD PCBs. The UMD PCBs
supply a voltage of the correct amplitude and polarity to rotate the gantry
motor in the speed and direction that the TCS commands.
The BRAKE and ENABLE signals are applied to the ROC-ICA PCB. This PCB
changes the ENABLE signal to a 0 V output, and changes the BRAKE signal to
a relay contact switch output. These signals are applied to the HME PCB.
If the control system is serviceable, the HME PCB has no effect on its inputs.
The ENABLE signal is applied to the UMD PCBs and the BRAKE signal is
routed to the gantry brake assembly. If there is a fault in the control system,
the HME PCB disconnects the ENABLE and BRAKE signals to the UMD PCBs.
Two UMD PCBs operate the gantry motors. The PCBs are connected in a
bridge. The SPEED/DIRECTION signal from the AO8-ICCA PCB controls the
speed of the gantry motors. The motors operate when they get the ENABLE
signal from the HME PCB. The motor drive PSU in Area 75 supplies the
±26 VDC power for the motors.
Servo signal The analog voltages from the primary and secondary encoders are made
processing smooth by the SCC PCBs and applied to the AI12 PCBs. These PCBs digitize
their analog inputs. The values that result are kept in the coarse, fine, and
check items that are read by the TCS.
A link between the encoder potentiometers gives an input to the DIE-ICB
PCB. The programmable electronic subsystem of this PCB changes the signal
and sends it as Item 490 Gantry cct to the TCS. This configuration makes sure
that the system cannot run with the potentiometers disconnected, which is a
possible 0 V condition.

4.11.1 VMAT - A description of the necessary position during VMAT dose
delivery
That is the geometric parameter movement during each ‘step’ controlled in relation to the
delivered dose. For example, if 1% of the dose is delivered, then 1% movement is completed, or if
2% of the dose is delivered then 2% of the movement is completed, if 99% of the dose is
delivered then 99% of the movement is completed.
For each cycle a new position for each geometry is calculated, this uses the delivered dose. For
movements of leaves and diaphragms the delivered dose is used in units of 1/64th MU. For
movements of gantry and collimator rotation, the delivered dose is used in units of 1/32nd MU.

4.11.2 VMAT - A description of the positional errors and tolerances
During each cycle, the software compares the positional error (instantaneous or averaged) against
a dynamic tolerance. If the positional error is more than the dynamic tolerance, then the software
disables the PRF which stops the radiation temporarily.
The instantaneous positional errors for leaves and diaphragms are averaged along a number of
cycles (this is currently equivalent to 1.4 seconds). The leaf and diaphragm errors are averaged
because there are no restrictions on changes in direction or speed. Therefore, with a direction
change at maximum speed there can be a ‘spike’ in the measured error. The leaf and diaphragm
errors are averaged to remove this ‘spike’.
The leaf/diaphragm dynamic tolerance (Part 225) is different from the leaf/diaphragm static
tolerance (set in the database).
With a dose resolution of 1/64th MU the leaf control algorithm can accurately control the leaf
position at dose rates to 50 MU/minute. Below this dose rate, accurate control cannot be
achieved. So for very low dose rates, of 25 MU/minute or less a larger tolerance is applied.
The gantry and collimator rotation errors are not averaged because the direction and speed
changes are set to limits by the linear accelerator server. The dynamic tolerance for the gantry
and collimator rotation are held in its ‘stop’ item, and the static tolerance is half this value.
All tolerances are specified in absolute distances, that is, that they are not specified as
percentages of distance to move.
When the radiation stops temporarily, the geometry items that are out of tolerance go back in
tolerance, at which point radiation starts again. The PRF disables for a maximum number of cycles
(currently equal to 25 seconds) before the HT disables, this shows an inhibit of Item 419 MLC Not
Ready. Termination of radiation is controlled by the action mask of Item 419 MLC Not Ready.

4.11.3 Description of dose delivery control
When the radiation stops temporarily, the geometry items that are out of tolerance go back in
tolerance and the radiation starts again.
The PRF will disable for a maximum number of cycles, the equivalent of 25 seconds, before the HT
will disable. This gives an Item 419 MLC Not Ready. The action mask of this inhibit controls the
beam termination.
4.11.4 VMAT - A description of the movement and speed demand
It is possible for the control system to move the geometry parameters faster than the published
maximum speeds. This lets a parameter ‘catch up’ if it falls behind when the necessary stable
movement speed is equal to, or is very close, to the published maximum speed.
For static steps the speed demand signal necessary to move the parameter into position is
calculated from the positional error and the proportional gain.
For dynamic steps, a feed-forward part has to be considered, even if the positional error for a
cycle is zero, a speed demand signal must be applied to monitor the necessary dynamic
movement above the subsequent cycle.
Note: The feed-forward part is always implemented in gantry/collimator rotation arc treatments. The
feed-forward movement is only put into effect during irradiation, if the radiation stops
temporarily, only the speed demand signal that results from the positional error is put into effect.
The feed-forward speed demand signal can cause a ‘go past’ of the necessary position. For
example, if radiation stops temporarily because a leaf is left behind, the gantry can ‘go past’ the
necessary position. The gantry control system thinks radiation is to be continuous and therefore
applies a feed-forward speed demand signal. If the radiation stops temporarily for a short time,
for example, one cycle, the ‘go past’ is corrected as movement continues. If the radiation stops
temporarily for a long time, the ‘go past’ is corrected by the positional error and can cause the
gantry movement in the opposite direction.
Gantry and collimator rotation movement is controlled by the algorithm used before Release
7.0x. The movement on Release 7.0x is used on the ‘step-arc’ and not the ‘segment-arc’. This lets
‘segment-arcs’ be delivered during change of speed.

4.11.5 VMAT - A description of the variable dose rate
The geometry parameters that move are leaves, diaphragms , gantry and collimator rotation.
Note: The Beam Modulator™ has diaphragms which do not move.
During VMAT radiation delivery, these four geometry parameters have to work together in order
to deliver the total radiation dose (in MU) per step.
For all fields where a geometric parameter is prescribed to move during radiation a dose rate will
be calculated by the control system. This calculation aims to minimize the number of beam hold-
offs caused a parameter failing to “keep up” with the delivered dose.
The maximum velocity of each geometry parameter can be seen in the Calibrate Movement
Speeds window:
In Service mode, click the Calibration primary function button.
Click the Calibrate Movement Speeds secondary function button.
In the Calibrate Movement Speeds window, the following information is displayed:
• Maximum gantry velocity (deg/s)
• Gantry inertia compensation distance (deg)
• Maximum collimator velocity (deg/s)
• Collimator inertia compensation distance (deg)
• Maximum leaf velocity (cm/s)
• Leaf inertia compensation distance (cm)
• Maximum diaphragm velocity (cm/s)
• Diaphragm inertia compensation distance (cm).
The default value for maximum velocity is the maximum speed of the movement. The Inertia
Compensation Distance (ICD) is the distance necessary for each movement to reach the
maximum velocity. The default value of the ICD is the recommended value for that the
movement.
Each of these geometry parameters have a maximum velocity at which they can move. Therefore,
the maximum velocity and the Inertia Compensation Distance (ICD)) for each movement is used
to calculate the necessary dose rate for each step of the VMAT radiation delivery.

4.11.6 VMAT - A description of the calculation of the necessary dose rates
and PRF codes
The dose rate and PRF code for each step is calculated as follows:
Ideal dose rate = Step dose / Minimum step time

Where:
Minimum step time is the maximum of:
Minimum dose time
Minimum gantry time
Minimum diaphragm travel time
Minimum collimator rotation time
Minimum leaf time.
Also:
Minimum dose time = Step dose / Maximum dose rate
Minimum gantry time = Gantry movement / Maximum gantry velocity
Minimum diaphragm travel time = Diaphragm travel / Maximum diaphragm velocity
Minimum collimator rotation time = Collimator rotation / Maximum diaphragm angular velocity.
For each leaf
Minimum Leaf Time = Leaf travel / Maximum leaf velocity.
The selected dose rate is the highest available dose rate which is not more than the ideal dose
rate. The available dose rates are calculated, by successive halving of the Maximum Dose Rate
(MDR) which is read from the Linac Configuration part. The maximum dose rate is obtained
through Item 44 D/rate 1 Part 134.

If a dose rate is specified, (that is, prescribed or overridden), the nearest available dose rate to
the specified dose rate, which is less than the ideal dose rate, is selected. If the specified dose
rate is equidistant from two available dose rates the higher dose rate is selected, (that is, the
specified dose rate is rounded up).

4.11.7 VMAT - A description of the Inertia Compensation Distance (ICD)
The geometry parameters do not always move at the maximum velocity. The geometry
parameters have inertia, that is, time is necessary for them to increase and decrease in speed.
The control system makes the adjustments for the inertia of geometry parameters. It causes them
to move temporarily faster than their maximum velocity until they are in the correct position.
The software takes account of the inertia compensation distance, when it calculates the
movement time for each geometry parameter.
For distances above the ICD the movement time is correctly specified as Distance / Maximum
velocity.
For distances less than ICD the movement time is approximate as the delivery time is not easily
predicted.
In the worst condition the movement time must be less than the time to go to the ICD (that is,
Inertia Compensation Distance / Maximum velocity). In the best condition the geometry item has
an initial velocity equal to the maximum velocity, in this case the movement time is Distance /
Maximum velocity.
The linear accelerator server does approximate between these two values based on the initial
velocity to estimate the movement time.
When a change of direction is necessary the angular velocity at start for the step is assumed to be
zero, the gantry is decreased in speed by the control system.

7.1 Doing a functional test of the gantry balance
Prerequisites
Equipment
• Ammeter
You must do a check of the gantry balance when:
• The linear accelerator is installed
• You do the planned maintenance procedures
• You do a repair, or other work, that changes the gantry balance.
1 In the gantry base (Area 50), connect the positive connection of an ammeter to TS 50 A-3.
2 Connect the negative connection of the ammeter to the red flying lead of the motor.
3 Rotate the gantry and read the motor current on the ammeter.
4 If it is necessary, add or remove the weights at the gantry weight stack (see Figure 7.1).
000469 ©2013 Elekta Limited
Figure 7.1 Gantry weights
5 Make sure that when the gantry rotates in each direction, the motor current is not more
than 2.5 A.
6 Record the motor current indications.
7 Make sure that the total current changes in the positive and negative direction by less than
±0.5 A.
8 In the gantry base, disconnect the ammeter.
7.2 Calibrating the gantry movement system
You must do the calibration of the gantry movement system as follows:
• After installation of the linear accelerator.
• As necessary during the planned maintenance procedures.
• After any repair of the gantry readback system.

7.3.1 Doing a functional check of the jockey wheels
The jockey wheels stop forward and rearward movement of the gantry drum during rotation.
1 Make sure that the linear accelerator is in a balanced condition.
2 Set the gantry arm to 180°.
3 Rotate the gantry through 360° and look at the jockey wheels.
4 Make sure that only two of the jockey wheels touch the rim of the gantry drum during
rotation.
5 Do steps 1 to 4 two more times.
6 If it is necessary, do steps 1 to 5 again.
7 If it is necessary, adjust the jockey wheels.
Related Links:
Doing the set up of the jockey wheels on page 133
7.3.2 Doing the set up of the jockey wheels
1 Make a clean, lint-free cloth moist with a cleaning agent, and clean the rim of the gantry
drum.
2 Move the gantry to the position at which all three jockey wheels touch the gantry drum at
the same time.
3 To adjust the jockey wheel on the gantry motor side of the gantry base, move the gantry
limit switch away from the jockey wheel.
4 Loosen one of the front adjustable jockey wheels.
5 Set a clearance of 0.2 mm between the jockey wheel and the rim of the gantry drum.
6 Rotate the gantry and make sure that only two jockey wheels are in contact with the gantry
drum.
7 If three jockey wheels touch the gantry drum, stop the rotation immediately.
8 Do 4 to 7 again, until only two jockey wheels are in contact with the gantry drum during
rotation.
9 If it is necessary, move the gantry limit switch to its initial position.

Prerequisites
Equipment
• Plumb line
1 Log on in Service Mode.

4 Rotate the gantry to -90° (+0/-1°).
5 Switch on the field lamp.
6 Use a plumb line across the face of the BLD.
7 Rotate the BLD until the edge of the diaphragm aligns with the plumb line. This sets the BLD
to 0° ±0.5°.
8 Make sure that the value of Item 561 Drot coars is 5.0 V (±100 mV).
9 If it is not, adjust the coarse potentiometer RV3 until the correct value is displayed.
10 Make sure that the value of Item 136 name D.rot. chk is 5.0 V (±100 mV).
11 If it is not, adjust the potentiometer RV1 until the correct value is displayed.
12 Rotate the BLD and make sure that the values of Item 561 Drot coars and Item 136 name
D.rot. chk change between 0.2 V and 9.8 V.

7.11 Calibrating the movement speeds
The TCS uses the maximum velocity and the ICD for each movement to calculate the dose rate for
each step of a VMAT delivery. The default value for maximum velocity is the maximum speed of
the movement. The ICD is the distance necessary for each movement to be at its maximum
velocity. The default value of the ICD is the recommended value for that movement.
Each of the geometry parameters have a maximum velocity at which they can move. Therefore,
the TCS uses the maximum velocity and the ICD for each movement to calculate the necessary
dose rate. The TCS does this for each step of the VMAT radiation delivery. When the movement
cannot keep up with the delivery, radiation delivery pauses. When this occurs the maximum
velocity or the ICD are too high, compared to the movement. You can decrease the values as a
temporary solution until you repair the movement speed.
1 Click the Calibration button.
2 Click the Calibrate Movement Speeds button.
The Calibrate Movement Speeds dialog box appears.
